svg.text: ANCIENT EGYPTIAN
GRAMMAR — AFROASIATIC STRUCTURE
EGYPTIAN LANGUAGE FAMILY
Egyptian: member of Afroasiatic family (same as Semitic, Berber, Cushitic)
Closest Afroasiatic relatives: Berber languages, then Semitic
Egyptian is NOT Semitic, but shares Afroasiatic features with Arabic/Hebrew
EGYPTIAN VERB SYSTEM
VSO word order (same as Classical Arabic and Biblical Hebrew)
Verbal sentence: sdm.f "he hears" = root S-D-M + subject suffix .f
Two genders: masculine and feminine (feminine = -t suffix)
Dual number: specific suffix for pairs (like Arabic/Hebrew)
EGYPTIAN NOUN SYSTEM
No case system (unlike Greek) • gender and number in nouns
Definite article: pa/ta/na (developed in Later Egyptian)
Coptic: pa/psi/pi = the (direct descendant)
Pronoun: subject encoded in verb suffix (like Semitic: sdm.f)
GREEK GRAMMAR · SECONDARY LANGUAGE
CLASSICAL GREEK CASE SYSTEM
5 cases (Ancient): nominative, accusative, genitive, dative, vocative
Case endings fully encode noun grammatical role (word order flexible)
Greek is highly inflected: noun, verb, adjective all inflect
GREEK ASPECT AND TENSE
Aspect-based: present (ongoing) / aorist (punctual) / perfect (state)
Plus tense (past vs non-past) on top of aspect = complex system
Aorist: no equivalent in most modern languages; "simple past without duration"
GRAMMAR COMPARISON
Egyptian: VSO, two genders, dual, verb-suffix pronouns (Afroasiatic)
Greek: flexible SVO/VSO, 3 genders, rich case system, aspect-based (IE)
Both: distinguished singular / dual / plural
Both: grammatical gender system governing agreement
Greek provided grammatical framework for Coptic (final Egyptian stage)
PLATE 61c ALT · ANCIENT EGYPTIAN / GREEK · GRAMMAR
